title: The World Before Transformers
subtitle: How a single 2017 paper rewrote the future of intelligence
theme: Understand the problem everyone was stuck on, and why 'Attention Is All You Need' was a thunderclap.
learningObjectives: Explain why sequence data (language, speech, time) is fundamentally hard for machines
Describe how RNNs, LSTMs, and seq2seq tried to solve it and where they broke down
Articulate the 'bottleneck problem' that crippled translation and long-context understanding
Understand the original attention idea (Bahdanau, 2014) as a fix bolted onto RNNs
Tell the story of the 2017 Transformer paper: who, where, why it was heretical, and what it dared to remove
Connect this origin story to every AI product the learner uses in June 2026
storyHook: In 2017, eight researchers at Google quietly published a paper with an almost arrogant title: 'Attention Is All You Need.' The machine-learning world had spent thirty years believing that to understand a sentence, a machine must read it word by word, in order, like a person dragging a finger across a page. This paper said: throw all of that away. What happened next did not just improve translation by a few percent. It set off the chain reaction that gave us ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, and the entire trillion-dollar AI economy you are living inside right now. Today we go back to the moment before the explosion.

slides.title: Welcome: Why We Start in 2017
slides.body:
## The 20-day journey begins

Welcome to **AI Mastery**. Over the next 20 days we are going to travel from a single 2017 research paper all the way to the AI frontier of **June 2026** — the world of reasoning models, agents, and AI that writes most of the code shipping today.

But you cannot understand where we are without understanding the wall everyone slammed into first.

### The big idea of Day 1

Every powerful AI system you touch today — the chatbot that drafts your emails, the model that summarizes a 200-page contract, the coding assistant inside your IDE — is a descendant of **one architecture**: the **Transformer**.

And the Transformer was born as a *rebellion*. It was a deliberate rejection of how machines had processed language for the previous three decades.

To feel why it mattered, we first have to sit inside the frustration of the researchers who came before it. We have to understand:

- Why **sequences** (language, audio, time-series) are uniquely hard
- Why the best tools of 2014–2016 (**RNNs, LSTMs, seq2seq**) kept hitting a ceiling
- What the **bottleneck** was that nobody could get past
- And why one paper's radical bet — *attention alone* — broke the dam

> By the end of today you will not just know the history. You will *feel* why this was a revolution.
slides.speakerNotes: Set the emotional stakes. This is not a dry history lecture — it's an origin story. Ask the room: 'How many of you used an AI tool this morning before walking in here?' Let hands go up. Then say: 'Every single one of those tools traces back to where we're standing today.' Promise them that by the end they'll understand the lineage of the tools they already depend on.
slides.marketConnection: As of June 2026, essentially every leading model — GPT-series, Gemini, Claude, Llama, Mistral, DeepSeek — is a Transformer or a Transformer variant. The architecture we study today is still the backbone of a market worth hundreds of billions.

slides.title: The Sequence Problem: Why Language Breaks Machines
slides.body:
## A picture is easy. A sentence is treachery.

By 2012, machines had become shockingly good at **images**. A neural network could look at a photo and say 'cat' with superhuman reliability. Why? Because an image is a *fixed, all-at-once* object. Every pixel is present simultaneously, and a convolutional network can scan the whole grid in parallel.

**Language is the opposite.** Language is a **sequence**. It unfolds *in time and order*, and meaning depends ruthlessly on that order:

- 'The dog bit the man.' vs. 'The man bit the dog.' — same words, opposite worlds.
- 'I never said she stole my money.' — this sentence has *seven different meanings* depending on which word you stress.

### The three curses of sequences

1. **Variable length.** A tweet is 5 words; a legal contract is 50,000. A machine needs to handle both with the same machinery.
2. **Long-range dependencies.** In 'The keys that I left on the kitchen table next to the bowl of fruit *are* missing,' the verb 'are' must agree with 'keys' — a word that appeared *eleven words earlier*. The machine has to *remember* across distance.
3. **Order is meaning.** Unlike a bag of ingredients, you cannot shuffle a sentence and keep its sense.

```
Image  → all pixels present at once   → process in PARALLEL (easy-ish)
Text   → words arrive one at a time   → must track ORDER + MEMORY (hard)
```

This is the **sequence problem**. Solving it well is the difference between a toy and a translator. For thirty years, the answer seemed obvious: build a machine that reads *one word at a time and remembers as it goes*. That answer was the **Recurrent Neural Network**.
slides.speakerNotes: Use the 'I never said she stole my money' example live — say it aloud seven times, stressing a different word each time, and watch the room realize each version means something different. This viscerally proves that order and emphasis carry meaning. Ask: 'If I gave you these words in a random order, could you recover the meaning?' The answer 'no' is the whole point.
slides.marketConnection: Even in 2026, the hardest benchmarks for frontier models are exactly the 'long-range dependency' ones — reasoning across a 1-million-token document where the key clue sits 400 pages before the question.

slides.title: The First Heroes: RNNs and the Idea of Memory
slides.body:
## A machine that reads with a finger on the page

The **Recurrent Neural Network (RNN)** was the elegant first answer. The intuition is beautiful and human: read the sentence **one word at a time**, and after each word, update a little internal scratchpad called the **hidden state**.

Think of it like reading a mystery novel. As you read each sentence, you update your mental summary of 'what's going on so far.' You don't re-read the whole book at every page; you carry a compressed memory forward.

```
word1 → [RNN] → h1 ─┐
word2 → [RNN] → h2 ─┤  each hidden state h carries
word3 → [RNN] → h3 ─┤  a summary of everything seen so far
word4 → [RNN] → h4 ─┘
```

Formally, the network computes a new hidden state from the **current word** plus the **previous hidden state**:

```
h_t = f( W * word_t  +  U * h_(t-1) )
```

The same weights `W` and `U` are reused at every step — that's the 'recurrent' part. One small machine, applied over and over down the line.

### Why this felt like magic in 2013

- It could handle **any length** — just keep looping.
- It maintained a **memory** that, in principle, summarized everything seen.
- It respected **order** by construction.

For a moment, it felt like the sequence problem was solved. But RNNs had a hidden disease — one that got worse the longer the sentence. To see it, we need to talk about what happens to memory over distance.
slides.speakerNotes: Emphasize the 'finger on the page' / reading-a-novel analogy. The key teaching move: the hidden state is a COMPRESSED running summary. Ask the class: 'When you read a long novel, how well do you remember the exact wording of chapter one by the time you reach chapter twenty?' This sets up the next slide perfectly — human memory fades over distance, and so did the RNN's.
slides.marketConnection: RNN-style thinking survives in 2026 in niche efficient-sequence models (state-space models like Mamba), which marketers position as cheaper alternatives to Transformers for very long streams — but they remain a small slice of the market.

slides.title: The Disease: Vanishing Gradients and Fading Memory
slides.body:
## Why the RNN forgot the beginning of its own sentence

The RNN's memory was real, but **leaky**. The further back a word was, the more its influence faded — like a whisper passed down a line of fifty people. By the end, the message is mush.

The technical name for this is the **vanishing gradient problem**.

### The intuition (no heavy math)

To learn, a network sends an error signal *backwards* through every step it took. In an RNN, that signal has to travel back through *every word*, getting multiplied by the same weights again and again. If those multipliers are slightly less than 1, the signal shrinks exponentially:

```
0.9^5  = 0.59      (5 words back: still ok)
0.9^20 = 0.12      (20 words back: faint)
0.9^50 = 0.005     (50 words back: gone)
```

The learning signal **vanishes** before it reaches the early words. So the network literally *cannot learn* dependencies across long distances. (The mirror problem, **exploding gradients**, blows the signal up to infinity — equally fatal.)

### What this meant in practice

- 'The keys ... *are* missing' — the RNN often picked the wrong verb because it had forgotten 'keys.'
- Translating a long sentence, the model lost the subject by the time it reached the end.

This was the great frustration of 2014. Researchers had a machine with memory, but its memory had **dementia**. The fix would be a cleverer cell — one engineered to *protect* important memories from fading. Enter the **LSTM**.
slides.speakerNotes: The 'whisper down a line of fifty people' analogy is gold — use it. Then write 0.9^50 on the board and let them compute roughly how tiny that is. The emotional beat: researchers had built memory, but it was a memory that forgot. Ask: 'What would you add to fix a memory that fades — some way to deliberately HOLD ON to important things?' That question literally describes the LSTM's gates.
slides.marketConnection: The vanishing-gradient lesson still echoes: a major 2025-2026 research thread is keeping signal 'alive' across million-token contexts. The problem changed shape but never fully disappeared.

slides.title: LSTMs: Giving the Machine a Gated Memory
slides.body:
## The cell that decided what to remember and what to forget

In 1997, long before deep learning was fashionable, Sepp Hochreiter and Jurgen Schmidhuber designed the **Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)** cell. It sat mostly unused for years — then, around 2014–2015, it became the workhorse of the entire field.

The LSTM's genius: instead of one fragile hidden state, it adds a protected **cell state** — a 'conveyor belt' of memory running straight through time — plus a set of little **gates** that decide what flows on and off the belt.

```
      ┌─────────── cell state (the memory conveyor belt) ───────────┐
      │                                                             │
   [forget gate] → [input gate] → [output gate]
   what to drop    what to add     what to reveal now
```

### The three gates, in plain English

- **Forget gate:** 'Is this old memory still relevant? If not, erase it.' (e.g., once a sentence ends, forget the old subject.)
- **Input gate:** 'Is this new word worth storing? If so, write it to memory.'
- **Output gate:** 'Given everything I'm holding, what should I expose to the next step right now?'

Because the cell state can flow forward *largely unchanged*, important information survives across many steps — the gradient no longer vanishes so easily. The dementia was treated.

### The golden age (2014–2016)

LSTMs (and their lighter cousins, **GRUs**) powered Google Translate, Siri's speech recognition, autocomplete, and early image captioning. They were genuinely state-of-the-art. For a couple of years, the world believed *this* was the final shape of sequence AI.

But LSTMs still carried one fatal inheritance from the RNN. They still read **one word at a time, strictly in order.** And that single constraint was about to collide with the realities of modern hardware — and with a translation bottleneck nobody could solve.
slides.speakerNotes: The 'conveyor belt with gates' image is the single most memorable way to teach LSTMs — draw it. Personify the gates: have them speak ('Forget that, it's old news!'). Then plant the seed of doom: 'LSTMs fixed memory, but they were still stuck reading word-by-word, one at a time. Why might that be a disaster when you have a giant GPU that can do thousands of things at once?' Hold that question.
slides.marketConnection: Almost no new product in 2026 is built on LSTMs — they're a museum piece for most use cases. But the LSTM 'gating' idea quietly lives on inside modern efficient architectures, a reminder that good ideas rarely die, they get absorbed.

slides.title: Seq2Seq and the Cruel Bottleneck
slides.body:
## The architecture that translated the world — and choked

Around 2014, Google and others combined LSTMs into a powerful pattern called **sequence-to-sequence (seq2seq)** — the design behind a massive leap in machine translation.

The idea is two machines holding hands:

1. An **encoder** LSTM reads the entire source sentence (say, French) word by word, building up its hidden state.
2. At the end, it compresses *everything* into a single fixed-size vector — the **context vector**, often called the 'thought vector.'
3. A **decoder** LSTM takes that one vector and generates the translation (English) word by word.

```
French: "Je suis tres heureux aujourd'hui"
   │ encoder reads it all...
   ▼
[ ONE fixed vector ]  ← ALL meaning squeezed into ~1000 numbers
   │ decoder reads only this...
   ▼
English: "I am very happy today"
```

### Spot the cruelty

The *entire meaning* of the source sentence — whether it's 5 words or 50 — has to be crushed into **one fixed-size vector**. This is the infamous **bottleneck problem**.

It's like asking someone to read a whole paragraph, then summarize it in a single fixed-length sentence *from memory*, and then asking a second person to reconstruct the original paragraph word-for-word **from only that summary.** For short sentences, fine. For long ones, catastrophe.

Researchers watched translation quality fall off a cliff as sentences grew longer. The model would translate the start beautifully and then drift, hallucinate, or simply forget the end of the source.

Everyone could *see* the problem. The whole field was stuck on the same wall: **how do you let the decoder look back at the source instead of relying on one squeezed-dry vector?** The answer would be a small, elegant idea called *attention*.
slides.speakerNotes: Make the bottleneck physical. Pick a volunteer: 'Read this paragraph, now summarize it in exactly one sentence, now I'll ask someone else to reproduce the paragraph from your sentence alone.' The failure is obvious and funny. That felt-failure IS the bottleneck. Then ask the pivotal question: 'What if, instead of relying on one summary, the translator could glance back at the original sentence whenever needed?' That is attention, and they'll have invented it themselves.
slides.marketConnection: The bottleneck lesson directly shaped the 2026 obsession with 'context windows.' When a vendor brags about a 1M or 2M token context, they are selling the exact opposite of the seq2seq bottleneck — the freedom to look back at everything.

slides.title: The Spark: Attention as a Rescue Mission (2014–2015)
slides.body:
## 'Let the model look back wherever it needs to'

In 2014–2015, Dzmitry Bahdanau, Kyunghyun Cho, and Yoshua Bengio published the idea that cracked the bottleneck: **attention**.

The insight is almost obvious in hindsight. Instead of forcing the decoder to rely on one squeezed context vector, **let it look back at every word of the source sentence** — and let it *decide which words matter most* for the word it's currently producing.

### How it works, intuitively

When the decoder is about to generate the English word for 'happy,' it asks: *'Which French words should I pay attention to right now?'* It computes a **relevance score** for every source word, turns those scores into weights that sum to 1, and builds a custom, **weighted blend** of the source — focused on 'heureux.'

```
Generating 'happy' → attention weights over source:
Je(0.02) suis(0.05) tres(0.20) heureux(0.70) aujourd'hui(0.03)
                                  ▲ the model 'looks' mostly here
```

For the *next* word it produces, the attention shifts to *different* source words. The model gets a **dynamic, content-based spotlight** that moves as it works.

### Why this was a big deal

- The bottleneck **dissolved** — no more cramming everything into one vector.
- Long-sentence translation **stopped collapsing.**
- Bonus: the attention weights were **interpretable** — you could literally draw which source word the model focused on for each output word, and it lined up with human intuition (a near word-for-word alignment).

But — and this is the crucial setup for our climax — attention in 2015 was still just a **helper bolted onto an LSTM**. The recurrent machine was still there underneath, still reading word by word, still slow. Attention was the passenger. The RNN was still driving.

Then someone asked the heretical question: *What if attention didn't need the RNN at all? What if attention could be the whole car?*
slides.speakerNotes: The word 'spotlight' is your best friend here — attention is a moving spotlight the model points at the most relevant words. Show the weights summing to 1 (it's a soft, weighted average, not a hard pick). Land the cliffhanger hard: 'In 2015, attention was just a helper. The RNN was still in charge. Now hold your breath — because someone is about to ask: what if we FIRE the RNN entirely?' That question is the whole revolution.
slides.marketConnection: Attention weights being interpretable seeded an entire 2026 industry: 'mechanistic interpretability' startups and safety teams that visualize what models attend to, sold to enterprises and regulators who need to trust AI decisions.

slides.title: 2017: 'Attention Is All You Need' — The Heretical Bet
slides.body:
## Eight people, one Google building, one outrageous claim

In June 2017, a team mostly at **Google Brain** and **Google Research** released a paper titled, with sly confidence, **'Attention Is All You Need.'** The authors: Ashish Vaswani, Noam Shazeer, Niki Parmar, Jakob Uszkoreit, Llion Jones, Aidan Gomez, Lukasz Kaiser, and Illia Polosukhin.

The context matters. Google ran **Google Translate** at planetary scale on LSTM-based seq2seq-with-attention. It worked, but it was **slow to train** and hard to scale, precisely because recurrence forces you to process words *one after another* — you can't compute word 50 until you've done words 1 through 49.

### The heresy

The team made a bet that sounded reckless: **remove the recurrence entirely.** No RNN. No LSTM. No reading word-by-word. Keep *only* the attention mechanism — and stack it.

This was genuinely heretical. The entire field's identity was built on the belief that **sequence understanding requires sequential processing.** Order had always been baked in by *processing in order*. The Transformer threw that away and said: we'll let every word look at every other word **all at once, in parallel**, and we'll inject order back in through a clever trick called **positional encoding** (Day 2).

```
OLD WAY (RNN/LSTM):  word1 → word2 → word3 → ...   (sequential, slow)
TRANSFORMER:         word1  word2  word3  ...        (all at once!)
                       └──── every word attends to every other ────┘
```

### Why it worked spectacularly

1. **Parallelism.** Because there's no waiting for the previous step, you can process an entire sentence simultaneously — a perfect match for the massively parallel **GPUs/TPUs** sitting idle under the old design. Training got *dramatically* faster.
2. **No distance penalty.** In a Transformer, word 1 and word 1000 are *one attention hop apart.* The vanishing-gradient curse of distance simply doesn't apply. Long-range dependencies become natural.
3. **It scaled.** And scaling, it turned out, was everything.

The paper's results beat the state of the art in translation while training in a fraction of the time. But almost nobody — including the authors — fully grasped that they had just lit the fuse on the largest technology wave of the next decade.
slides.speakerNotes: Tell this like a heist story. Set the scene: Google Translate is huge, but recurrence makes it slow and unscalable. Eight people make a bet that violates the field's deepest assumption. Emphasize the word 'heretical' — the whole field believed order required sequential reading. The punchline: by removing recurrence they unlocked the GPU. Ask the room: 'Why would processing all words at once instead of one-by-one be a massive deal for a chip with thousands of cores?' Let them connect parallelism to hardware.
slides.marketConnection: Every frontier lab in June 2026 — OpenAI, Google DeepMind, Anthropic, Meta, xAI, DeepSeek — builds on this 2017 architecture. The 'parallelism unlocks scale' insight is the entire reason GPU maker NVIDIA became one of the most valuable companies on Earth.

slides.title: Why It Changed EVERYTHING: From Paper to ChatGPT
slides.body:
## The fuse that took five years to detonate

The Transformer didn't just win at translation. It quietly unlocked a recipe: **scale this thing up and feed it the internet.**

Because Transformers train in parallel, you can make them *enormous* and train them on *staggering* amounts of text without recurrence choking you. That single property kicked off a cascade:

- **2018 — BERT (Google):** Took the Transformer *encoder*, trained it to fill in blanked-out words, and shattered records across language understanding. Search got smarter overnight.
- **2018-2019 — GPT / GPT-2 (OpenAI):** Took the Transformer *decoder* and trained it to predict the next word. GPT-2 wrote eerily coherent paragraphs.
- **2020 — GPT-3:** 175 billion parameters. Suddenly the model could write essays, code, and answer questions it was never explicitly taught — just from scale.
- **Nov 2022 — ChatGPT:** The moment the public met the Transformer. 100 million users in two months. The fastest-adopted product in history.
- **2023-2026 — the explosion:** GPT-4, Gemini, Claude, Llama, the rise of *multimodal* models (text + image + audio + video), *reasoning* models that think step-by-step, and *agents* that take actions. All Transformers at the core.

```
2017 Transformer
   │
   ├── encoder branch → BERT → modern search & understanding
   ├── decoder branch → GPT → ChatGPT → the generative AI boom
   └── both → translation, vision, audio, code, agents (2026)
```

### The deeper lesson

The revolution wasn't a clever trick. It was the discovery of an architecture that **gets better the more you scale it** — more data, more parameters, more compute — without falling apart. That property, called **scalability**, is why a 2017 translation paper became the foundation of a global industry. The researchers removed one assumption — 'sequences must be processed sequentially' — and in doing so, removed the ceiling.
slides.speakerNotes: This is the emotional payoff of Day 1 — deliver it with energy. Walk the timeline from 2017 to 2026 as a runaway chain reaction. The key intellectual takeaway: the magic wasn't a single trick, it was SCALABILITY — an architecture that keeps improving as you pour in more compute. Ask: 'What do BERT and GPT have in common, and what's the one thing they each kept from the 2017 paper?' (Answer: attention; one took the encoder, one took the decoder.)
slides.marketConnection: In June 2026, the 'just scale it' thesis is being actively debated — labs now blend scale with reasoning, tool-use, and agents. But the Transformer remains the substrate under all of it. The entire trillion-dollar AI capex race is downstream of this one architecture.

slides.title: Recap: The Road From Memory to Attention
slides.body:
## The story in one arc

We traveled the full pre-history of modern AI today. Let's lock it in.

### The journey

```
The sequence problem        → language is ordered, variable-length, long-range
        ↓
RNNs                        → read word-by-word, carry a memory (but it fades)
        ↓
Vanishing gradients         → memory has 'dementia' over distance
        ↓
LSTMs                       → gated memory conveyor belt (fixes fading)
        ↓
Seq2seq                     → encoder→ONE vector→decoder = the BOTTLENECK
        ↓
Attention (2015)            → let the decoder look back; a moving spotlight
        ↓
Transformer (2017)          → fire the RNN; attention ALONE, all words at once
        ↓
BERT, GPT, ChatGPT → 2026   → the entire modern AI industry
```

### The three breakthroughs that mattered most

1. **Memory** (LSTM gates): how to hold information over time.
2. **Attention** (2015): how to dynamically focus on what's relevant.
3. **Pure parallel attention** (2017 Transformer): how to do it *all at once*, unlocking scale.

### The one sentence to remember

> The Transformer's revolution was removing recurrence — letting every word attend to every other word **simultaneously** — which unlocked GPU-scale training and, with it, the entire age of large language models.

Tomorrow we crack open the Transformer itself and see exactly *how* attention works — the famous Q, K, V — with a concrete numeric example you'll be able to compute by hand.
slides.speakerNotes: Walk the full arrow-diagram from top to bottom out loud — repetition cements the arc. Then ask the class to reconstruct it with you, prompting: 'What came after seq2seq, and what problem did it solve?' Make them retrieve it; retrieval beats re-reading for memory. Close by hyping Day 2: tomorrow they'll actually DO the attention math with small numbers.
slides.marketConnection: Understanding this lineage is a practical 2026 skill: when a vendor pitches a 'non-Transformer breakthrough' (state-space models, diffusion-LLMs), you can now ask the right question — does it beat the Transformer on scalability, the very property that made 2017 matter?

slides.title: Quick Check: Did the Story Land?
slides.body:
## Five questions to test your grip on Day 1

Discuss in pairs, then we'll review as a room.

**1. The bottleneck.** In seq2seq, what exactly was the 'bottleneck,' and why did it hurt long sentences more than short ones?

**2. Memory's disease.** What is the vanishing gradient problem, and how did the LSTM's gates help fight it?

**3. The spotlight.** In one sentence, what does an attention mechanism let a model do that a single context vector did not?

**4. The heresy.** What did the 2017 Transformer paper *remove* that the entire field believed was essential — and what did it gain by removing it?

**5. The cascade.** Name one model that took the Transformer *encoder* and one that took the *decoder*, and what each became known for.

---

### Bonus market question

When a 2026 AI vendor advertises a '2 million token context window,' which historical problem from today's lesson are they directly selling you the cure for?

*(Answer to bonus: the seq2seq bottleneck — the freedom to look back at huge amounts of source material instead of cramming it into one fixed vector.)*
slides.speakerNotes: Run this as think-pair-share, not a cold quiz — it lowers anxiety and surfaces misconceptions. Spend the most time on Q4 (the heresy) because it's the heart of the day. For the bonus, connect it explicitly to a tool they use. Reward good answers and gently correct the common mistake that 'attention replaced memory' — clarify attention replaced the RECURRENCE/bottleneck, not the idea of context.
slides.marketConnection: These exact concepts — context windows, attention, scaling — are the vocabulary of every 2026 AI product launch and earnings call. Knowing them lets learners read the market like insiders.

keyTakeaways: Language is a sequence: ordered, variable-length, and full of long-range dependencies — which is what makes it hard for machines.
RNNs introduced memory but suffered vanishing gradients; LSTMs fixed fading memory with gates but still read strictly word-by-word.
Seq2seq crammed an entire sentence's meaning into one fixed vector — the bottleneck that broke long-sentence translation.
Attention (2015) solved the bottleneck by letting the model look back and focus dynamically — a moving spotlight over the input.
The 2017 Transformer's heresy was removing recurrence entirely, letting all words attend in parallel — which unlocked GPU-scale training.
Scalability, not a single trick, is why one 2017 paper became the foundation of BERT, GPT, ChatGPT, and the entire 2026 AI industry.
marketSnapshot2026: As of June 2026, the Transformer born in this 2017 paper is still the undisputed backbone of the AI economy. Every frontier system — OpenAI's GPT line, Google's Gemini, Anthropic's Claude, Meta's Llama, xAI's Grok, and the surging open models from DeepSeek and Mistral — is a Transformer or close variant. The 'parallelism unlocks scale' insight is the reason NVIDIA became one of the most valuable companies on the planet and why hundreds of billions in capital are flowing into GPU data centers. The marketing language you hear daily — 'context windows,' 'attention,' 'scaling laws,' 'multimodal' — is all vocabulary from the story we told today. Even the challengers (state-space models like Mamba, diffusion-based text models) define themselves *against* the Transformer, which tells you exactly how central it remains.
homework: Pick one AI product you used this week (a chatbot, a coding assistant, a translator, a summarizer). Write half a page answering: (1) Which historical problem from today — bottleneck, fading memory, or sequential slowness — would this product have suffered from in 2016? (2) How does its modern Transformer foundation let it avoid that problem now? Bring it to Day 2.
nextDayTeaser: Tomorrow we open the machine itself. You'll meet the legendary Q, K, and V — Query, Key, and Value — and finally understand *how* a Transformer pays attention, step by step, with a small numeric example you can work out by hand. By the end of Day 2, the most famous diagram in modern AI will feel like an old friend.
